title: Tuberculosis — Pulmonary
oneLiner: A chronic necrotizing lung infection, primarily caused by *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, presenting with cough, haemoptysis, and night sweats; requires a high index of suspicion.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 32-year-old male, recent immigrant from India, presents with a 6-week history of persistent cough, initially dry then productive of green sputum with occasional streaks of blood. He reports drenching night sweats, unintentional weight loss of 5kg, and low-grade fevers. He has a 10-pack-year smoking history. On examination, he is thin, afebrile in clinic, but has dullness to percussion and reduced breath sounds at the left apex. CXR shows an apical opacity with central cavitation.

**Pattern Clues Dissected:**
- **Persistent cough > 3 weeks:** TB should be suspected in any cough lasting >3 weeks, especially with risk factors.
- **Haemoptysis (streaks):** Indicates bronchial mucosal inflammation or erosion into a small vessel – classic for active TB.
- **Night sweats & weight loss:** Constitutional symptoms – driven by TNF-alpha and IL-1 release from activated macrophages.
- **Recent immigrant from high-prevalence region (India):** TB is endemic in many parts of the world; ask about travel, prison, homeless shelters.
- **Cavitation (CXR):** Represents caseous necrosis and liquefaction – hallmark of reactivation TB in a post-primary infection. Cavitary lesions are highly infectious.
- **Apical predilection:** Upper lobes have higher oxygen tension (PaO2 ~100 mmHg) which favours growth of *M. tuberculosis* (obligate aerobe).

**Atypical Presentations (may trick you):**
- **Elderly patient with minimal cough, low-grade fever, altered mental status:** TB can present as indolent disease with only weight loss and confusion – may mimic malignancy or dementia.
- **HIV-positive patient (CD4 <200) with negative CXR:** Pulmonary TB may cause a miliary pattern (tiny nodules) or even a normal CXR. Sputum AFB may be negative; rely on culture and molecular tests.
- **Diabetic patient with bilateral lower lobe infiltrates:** Diabetes impairs cell-mediated immunity; reactivation can occur in lower lobes, mimicking community-acquired pneumonia.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-Language Definition:**  
Pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) is a bacterial lung infection caused by *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*. It is transmitted via airborne droplets from patients with active pulmonary disease. The body’s immune response walls off the infection, but reactivation can occur years later when immunity wanes.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**
- **Frequency:** 10.6 million new cases globally (2021). In low-incidence countries like UK/US, still a significant infection in immigrant, homeless, and HIV-positive populations.
- **Stakes:** If untreated, ~50% die within 5 years. Also highly infectious – a single smear-positive case can infect 10–15 contacts per year.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Missed diagnosis due to atypical presentation, false-negative sputum AFB, or assuming “atypical mycobacteria” without species identification. Treatment mismanagement (especially drug resistance) leads to relapse and broader transmission.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The Story of TB Infection:**

1. **Inhalation:** Droplet nuclei (1–5 μm) containing *M. tuberculosis* are inhaled into alveoli. The bacteria are phagocytosed by alveolar macrophages but survive by inhibiting phagosome-lysosome fusion.

2. **Primary Infection – Ghon Complex:** In the immunocompetent host, a small parenchymal lesion develops (Ghon focus) with hilar lymphadenopathy (Ghon complex). This is asymptomatic in 90% and usually heals with fibrosis or calcification.

3. **Latent TB Infection (LTBI):** Bacteria persist in a dormant state inside granulomas – a mixture of macrophages, epithelioid cells, and Langhans giant cells. The host remains asymptomatic, and the chest X-ray may be normal or show a calcified granuloma.

4. **Reactivation (Post-Primary TB):** Happens when cell-mediated immunity declines (e.g. HIV, steroids, aging). The necrotic centre of the granuloma undergoes caseation, liquefies, and erodes into a bronchus – releasing bacteria into the airways.

**Why Clinical Signs Happen:**

| Sign/Findling | Mechanism |
|---------------|-----------|
| **Cough** (productive) | Bronchial irritation from caseous material and inflammation. |
| **Haemoptysis** | Erosion into bronchial arteries (high pressure) or post-tubercular bronchiectasis. |
| **Night sweats & fatigue** | Cytokine-mediated (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α) affecting hypothalamus – fever and sweating. |
| **Cavitation on CXR** | Liquefactive necrosis + bronchial communication. Air in cavity gives radiolucent halo. |
| **Apical location** | High partial pressure of oxygen in upper lobes – aerobic bacteria thrive. Also poor lymphatic clearance. |
| **Granuloma** | IFN-γ from Th1 cells activates macrophages to form epithelioid cells; giant cells attempt to contain the bacteria. |

**Connecting to Pattern Recognition:** The classic vigenette’s apical cavitation and night sweats are direct results of this pathophys. Cavitation also explains high contagiousness – cough generates aerosolised bacteria.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Infectious – most common** | *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* (human) | Contact with active TB case; positive IGRA |
| **Infectious** | *M. bovis* (bovine) | Unpasteurised dairy products; cervical lymphadenopathy |
| **Reactivation triggers** | HIV infection (CD4 <350) | Low CD4 count, oral thrush, weight loss |
| **Reactivation triggers** | TNF-α inhibitors (e.g., infliximab) | Patient with rheumatoid arthritis on biologic |
| **Reactivation triggers** | Immunosuppressants (steroids, post-transplant) | Chronic steroid use, organ transplant recipient |
| **Reactivation triggers** | Malnutrition, diabetes, chronic renal failure | Poor dentition, dialysis, low albumin |
| **Reactivation triggers** | Silicosis | Occupational exposure + CXR with upper lobe nodules |
| **Latent infection** | *M. tuberculosis* – no active disease | Positive TST/IGRA, normal CXR, asymptomatic |

**Most common cause:** Reactivation of **latent *M. tuberculosis* infection** – up to 5–10% lifetime risk in immunocompetent, higher in immunosuppressed.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria:**
- **Clinical (symptoms + risk factors)** + **microbiological (AFB smear, culture, molecular)**
- **Standard:** At least **2 sputum samples** for AFB smear and culture, plus chest X-ray.
- **Scoring?** Not routinely used – but **NICE guidelines** recommend culture-positive case definition.

**Key Tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Chest X-ray** | Apical infiltrates, cavitation, hilar lymphadenopathy (primary), fibrosis, pleural effusion | First-line screening; pattern recognition | Normal – does not rule out (especially HIV). Cavitation = high infectivity. Upper lobe = classic. |
| **Sputum AFB smear (Ziehl-Neelsen)** | Red acid-fast bacilli (AFB) on microscopy | Rapid result (<24h) to confirm TB and assess infectivity | + smear = high bacterial load, infectious. Negative smear ≠ not TB (paucibacillary disease). |
| **GeneXpert (MTB/RIF)** | Detects *M. tuberculosis* DNA + rifampicin resistance mutations | Molecular test with same-day result; also RIF resistance screening | Positive + RIF resistance = MDR-TB suspect. Negative = low probability but not excluded. |
| **Mycobacterial culture (liquid + solid)** | Growth of *M. tuberculosis* (2–6 weeks) | Gold standard for confirmation and full DST | Positive = definite diagnosis. DST guides therapy. |
| **Interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA)** | T-cell response to TB-specific antigens | Latent TB diagnosis; helps in screening | Positive = infection (active or latent). Negative suggests no infection (but false-negative in immunosuppression). |
| **Bronchoscopy with BAL** | Direct sampling from affected lobe | If sputum smear-negative or patient cannot expectorate | BAL smear + culture + molecular – high yield in cavitary disease. |

**Single Finding That Confirms:** **Positive culture for *M. tuberculosis*** from any respiratory specimen.

**Single Finding That Rules Out (with caveats):** Three negative sputum smears + negative GeneXpert + no clinical/CXR evidence of TB makes active TB **unlikely** but not impossible (paucibacillary, atypical TB). For latent TB, negative IGRA essentially rules out infection except in severe immunosuppression.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (First Hour):**
- **Infection control:** Isolate the patient in a **negative pressure room** (airborne precautions: N95 respirator, surgical mask on patient).
- **Obtain 3 sputum samples** (if not already done) for AFB, GeneXpert, culture.
- **Initiate therapy** *if suspicion is high* (e.g., cavitary CXR + positive smear). Do not wait for culture if risk of infectivity or severe disease.

**Ward (24–72 Hours):**
- **Start standard 4-drug regimen:**
  - **Rifampicin** 600 mg PO once daily (if <50 kg: 10 mg/kg)
  - **Isoniazid** 300 mg PO once daily (pyridoxine 25 mg co-administered to prevent neuropathy)
  - **Pyrazinamide** 1.5–2 g PO once daily (<50 kg: 30 mg/kg)
  - **Ethambutol** 15 mg/kg PO once daily
  *Duration:* 2 months (intensive phase) → then 4 months continuation (Rifampicin + Isoniazid). Total 6 months. For cavitary disease or culture-positive at 2 months → extend to 9 months.

- **Check baseline:** LFTs (Rifampicin/Isoniazid), uric acid (Pyrazinamide), visual acuity/colour vision (Ethambutol), renal function.
- **Start pyridoxine (vit B6)** 25 mg daily with Isoniazid to prevent peripheral neuropathy.
- **Advise:** All drugs taken together empty stomach (1 hour before food) – Rifampicin absorption reduced with food.

- **Monitor adherence via Directly Observed Therapy (DOT)** – especially if social risk factors, homelessness, substance use. Consider fixed-dose combination tablets (FDC) to reduce pill burden.

- **Why these drugs?** Rifampicin and Isoniazid are the two most potent bactericidal agents. Pyrazinamide works in acidic intracellular environments. Ethambutol prevents emergence of resistance during intensive phase (especially if rifampicin resistance unknown).

**Long-Term:**
- **Continuation phase (months 3–6):** Rifampicin + Isoniazid daily (or three times weekly under DOT).
- **Monitor for hepatotoxicity:** monthly LFTs, clinical signs (nausea, jaundice, dark urine). If ALT >3x normal with symptoms or >5x without, hold drugs and discuss with TB specialist.
- **Screen for peripheral neuropathy** at each visit (Isoniazid). Adjust pyridoxine dose to 50–100 mg if symptoms occur.
- **Repeat sputum culture at month 2** – if still positive, check drug sensitivity, consider extended treatment.
- **Complete treatment = 6 months (or longer if MDR, CNS involvement, etc.).**

**What NOT to do (Common Pitfalls):**
- **Use of fluoroquinolones alone** for suspected community-acquired pneumonia when TB is possible – can delay diagnosis and mask disease.
- **Do not start steroids routinely** for pulmonary TB unless tuberculosis pericarditis or severe respiratory failure (acute distress syndrome) – steroids can worsen bacterial clearance.
- **Do not use isoniazid alone** for secondary prevention in active TB – it causes rapid emergence of resistance.
- **Do not stop treatment** just because patient feels better – relapse risk ~10% if <6 months.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Pulmonary TB | Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP) | Lung Cancer | Sarcoidosis |
|---------|--------------|-------------------------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Pain** | Usually none; pleuritic if effusion | Pleuritic chest pain common | Dull ache, may be absent | Rare; if hilar nodes, chest pressure |
| **Exam** | Dullness at apex; crackles; lymphadenopathy (supraclavicular) | Bronchial breathing, coarse crackles | Usually normal until late; clubbing | Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, skin lesions (erythema nodosum) |
| **CXR** | Apical cavity, upper lobe opacity | Lobar consolidation (air bronchogram) | Solitary nodule, mass, pleural effusion | Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, pulmonary infiltrates (Stage I–III) |
| **Symptoms** | Cough >3 weeks, night sweats, weight loss | Acute onset (<1 week), high fever, purulent sputum | Chronic cough, haemoptysis (frothy or clot), weight loss | Fatigue, dry cough, ocular symptoms, arthralgia |
| **Labs** | AFB smear +, GeneXpert +, lymphopenia, high ESR/CRP | Leukocytosis, procalcitonin elevated | Normal CBC, LDH elevated, Ca2+ may be high (paraneoplastic) | ACE level elevated (not specific), hypercalcaemia, lymphopenia |
| **Key differentiating test** | Sputum AFB + culture positive | Blood cultures, sputum Gram stain/culture | CT chest + PET, biopsy (bronchoscopy) | Lymph node biopsy (non-caseating granulomas), negative TB work-up |

**Mimics recap:** TB can be mistaken for **pneumonia** (acute vs chronic), **lung cancer** (weight loss, haemoptysis, apical mass – but TB cavity is thin-walled), and **sarcoidosis** (bilateral hilar nodes, but TB rarely causes bilateral nodes unless miliary).

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | Action |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|--------|
| **Haemoptysis (massive)** | Coughing up >200 mL blood in 24h; significant airway obstruction or shock | Hypotension, tachypnoea, oxygen desaturation; CXR shows new cavitary lesion | Emergency: call bronchoscopy/IR ± embolisation. Give supplemental O2, cross-match, IV fluids. |
| **Bronchiectasis** | Chronic cough with copious purulent sputum, haemoptysis, recurrent infections | Chest crackles, clubbing, CXR: tram track lines, HRCT shows bronchiectasis | Treat exacerbations with antibiotics; consider lung resection if limited disease. |
| **Miliary TB** | Disseminated via bloodstream: high fever, hepatosplenomegaly, meningitis, choroidal tubercles | CXR: millet-sized nodules bilaterally; elevated LFTs, hyponatremia (SIADH) | Start full TB therapy immediately; consider steroids for severe CNS involvement. |
| **Tuberculous pleural effusion** | Shortness of breath, pleuritic pain, dull percussion, decreased breath sounds | CXR: blunted costophrenic angle; pleural fluid: exudative (protein >3 g/dL), lymphocytic (>50%), ADA high | Diagnostic thoracentesis – start TB therapy; steroids not indicated unless adhesions. |
| **Laryngeal TB** | Hoarseness, odynophagia, ear pain (referred from vagus nerve) | Laryngoscopy shows mucosal ulcerations or granulomas | Ask about TB symptoms; treat as pulmonary TB; obtain sputum for AFB. |
| **TB meningitis** (late, untreated) | Headache, fever, neck stiffness, drowsiness, cranial nerve palsies (especially VI) | CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, low glucose, high protein | Urgent – start TB therapy + dexamethasone; LP confirms. High mortality if delayed. |
| **Drug-induced hepatitis** | Nausea, jaundice, dark urine, RUQ pain | LFTs: ALT >3x ULN + symptoms | Hold all hepatotoxic drugs (Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide) and discuss with TB specialist; alternative regimen needed. |

**Late presentation pattern:** Untreated pulmonary TB steadily worsens – weight loss becomes cachexia, haemoptysis can be fatal (Rasmussen aneurysm from cavitation eroding a pulmonary artery branch), and miliary spread leads to multiorgan failure.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic – RIPE for first-line drugs:**  
- **R**ifampicin  
- **I**soniazid  
- **P**yrazinamide  
- **E**thambutol  

**Mnemonic – SIDE effects of TB drugs:**
- **R**ifampicin: **R**ed urine, **R**ash, **R**ise in LFTs, **R**apid metabolism of other drugs (war, OCP, etc.)
- **I**soniazid: **I**nflammation of liver, **I**nsufficiency of pyridoxine → neuropathy
- **P**yrazinamide: **P**ain in joints (hyperuricemia)
- **E**thambutol: **E**yes – optic neuritis (test colour vision monthly)

**Mnemonic – Who to screen for latent TB?**
- **P**rison inmates
- **H**IV-positive
- **I**mmigrant from high-prevalence country
- **S**ilicosis
- **C**ontact of active case
- **T**NF-alpha inhibitor candidates

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls:**
1. **"Think TB when cough >3 weeks + any risk factor."** Even without classic symptoms.
2. **Cavitation on CXR is almost always TB until proven otherwise** (especially in young adults). Lung cancer cavities are thick-walled with irregular margins.
3. **Negative sputum AFB does not rule out TB** – especially in HIV, children, or extra-pulmonary disease. Send GeneXpert.
4. **Check for drug interactions with Rifampicin:** It potently induces CYP3A4, reducing levels of warfarin, oral contraceptives, methadone, antiretrovirals.
5. **Pyridoxine is mandatory with Isoniazid** to prevent peripheral neuropathy – but dose of 10–25 mg/day is enough for prophylaxis.
6. **Do not stop rifampicin without discussing with TB specialist** – it is the cornerstone of treatment and resistance can be missed.
7. **Weight-based dosing** is essential for Ethambutol and Pyrazinamide – avoid fixed doses in extremes of weight (<50 kg or >80 kg).

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**
- **Ordering TB tests without clinical suspicion** (low yield – leads to false positives in low-prevalence settings)
- **Using steroids alone for TB pericarditis** without anti-TB therapy
- **Starting quinolone monotherapy** for a COPD exacerbation (may suppress AFB smears and delay culture)
- **Failing to isolate the patient** when AFB smear-positive – risk of nosocomial spread

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
| Scenario | Threshold | Action |
|----------|-----------|--------|
| **Massive haemoptysis** | >200 mL in 24h or airway compromise + haemodynamic instability | Call anaesthetics/ICU, bronchoscopy team, interventional radiology for bronchial artery embolisation. |
| **Respiratory failure** | SpO2 <90% on room air, NEWS2 ≥5, PaO2 <8 kPa on ABG (type 1/2) | High-flow O2, consider NIV if hypercapnoeic, transfer to ICU. |
| **TB meningitis** | New confusion, GCS ≤14, seizure, or focal neurology | Urgent CT head + LP. Start dexamethasone + anti-TB; refer to neurology/infectious disease; ICU if reduced consciousness. |
| **Drug-induced hepatitis** | ALT >5x ULN (even asymptomatic) or >3x ULN with symptoms (nausea, jaundice) | Stop all drugs, check INR. Contact TB specialist. Replace with alternative regimen (e.g., streptomycin, levofloxacin, etc.). May need admission. |
| **Sepsis (miliary TB)** | NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >2 mmol/L, hypotension not responding to fluid | Full sepsis protocol, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (while awaiting TB results). Start TB therapy as soon as suspected. ICU review. |
| **Hepatotoxicity requiring hold** | Any sign of jaundice or ALT rising >5x ULN | Call senior clinician, stop hepatotoxic agents immediately, arrange daily LFTs, discuss with ID/microbiology. |

**Key thresholds:**  
- NEWS2 >5 triggers escalation review.  
- GCS <12 triggers CT head and consideration for ICU.  
- Lactate >4 mmol/L in any setting (especially with miliary pattern) indicates severe sepsis.  
- ALT >200 U/L = immediate hepatotoxicity work-up.

**Always communicate:** “I have a patient with suspected/confirmed TB who is deteriorating due to [complication]. I need a senior review within [minutes] because [specific parameter] is compromised.”

## Tuberculosis — Pulmonary

**One-liner:** A chronic necrotizing lung infection, primarily caused by *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*, presenting with cough, haemoptysis, and night sweats; requires a high index of suspicion.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 32-year-old male, recent immigrant from India, presents with a 6-week history of persistent cough, initially dry then productive of green sputum with occasional streaks of blood. He reports drenching night sweats, unintentional weight loss of 5kg, and low-grade fevers. He has a 10-pack-year smoking history. On examination, he is thin, afebrile in clinic, but has dullness to percussion and reduced breath sounds at the left apex. CXR shows an apical opacity with central cavitation.

**Pattern Clues Dissected:**
- **Persistent cough > 3 weeks:** TB should be suspected in any cough lasting >3 weeks, especially with risk factors.
- **Haemoptysis (streaks):** Indicates bronchial mucosal inflammation or erosion into a small vessel – classic for active TB.
- **Night sweats & weight loss:** Constitutional symptoms – driven by TNF-alpha and IL-1 release from activated macrophages.
- **Recent immigrant from high-prevalence region (India):** TB is endemic in many parts of the world; ask about travel, prison, homeless shelters.
- **Cavitation (CXR):** Represents caseous necrosis and liquefaction – hallmark of reactivation TB in a post-primary infection. Cavitary lesions are highly infectious.
- **Apical predilection:** Upper lobes have higher oxygen tension (PaO2 ~100 mmHg) which favours growth of *M. tuberculosis* (obligate aerobe).

**Atypical Presentations (may trick you):**
- **Elderly patient with minimal cough, low-grade fever, altered mental status:** TB can present as indolent disease with only weight loss and confusion – may mimic malignancy or dementia.
- **HIV-positive patient (CD4 <200) with negative CXR:** Pulmonary TB may cause a miliary pattern (tiny nodules) or even a normal CXR. Sputum AFB may be negative; rely on culture and molecular tests.
- **Diabetic patient with bilateral lower lobe infiltrates:** Diabetes impairs cell-mediated immunity; reactivation can occur in lower lobes, mimicking community-acquired pneumonia.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-Language Definition:**  
Pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) is a bacterial lung infection caused by *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*. It is transmitted via airborne droplets from patients with active pulmonary disease. The body’s immune response walls off the infection, but reactivation can occur years later when immunity wanes.

**Why It Matters Clinically:**
- **Frequency:** 10.6 million new cases globally (2021). In low-incidence countries like UK/US, still a significant infection in immigrant, homeless, and HIV-positive populations.
- **Stakes:** If untreated, ~50% die within 5 years. Also highly infectious – a single smear-positive case can infect 10–15 contacts per year.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Missed diagnosis due to atypical presentation, false-negative sputum AFB, or assuming “atypical mycobacteria” without species identification. Treatment mismanagement (especially drug resistance) leads to relapse and broader transmission.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The Story of TB Infection:**

1. **Inhalation:** Droplet nuclei (1–5 μm) containing *M. tuberculosis* are inhaled into alveoli. The bacteria are phagocytosed by alveolar macrophages but survive by inhibiting phagosome-lysosome fusion.

2. **Primary Infection – Ghon Complex:** In the immunocompetent host, a small parenchymal lesion develops (Ghon focus) with hilar lymphadenopathy (Ghon complex). This is asymptomatic in 90% and usually heals with fibrosis or calcification.

3. **Latent TB Infection (LTBI):** Bacteria persist in a dormant state inside granulomas – a mixture of macrophages, epithelioid cells, and Langhans giant cells. The host remains asymptomatic, and the chest X-ray may be normal or show a calcified granuloma.

4. **Reactivation (Post-Primary TB):** Happens when cell-mediated immunity declines (e.g. HIV, steroids, aging). The necrotic centre of the granuloma undergoes caseation, liquefies, and erodes into a bronchus – releasing bacteria into the airways.

**Why Clinical Signs Happen:**

| Sign/Findling | Mechanism |
|---------------|-----------|
| **Cough** (productive) | Bronchial irritation from caseous material and inflammation. |
| **Haemoptysis** | Erosion into bronchial arteries (high pressure) or post-tubercular bronchiectasis. |
| **Night sweats & fatigue** | Cytokine-mediated (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α) affecting hypothalamus – fever and sweating. |
| **Cavitation on CXR** | Liquefactive necrosis + bronchial communication. Air in cavity gives radiolucent halo. |
| **Apical location** | High partial pressure of oxygen in upper lobes – aerobic bacteria thrive. Also poor lymphatic clearance. |
| **Granuloma** | IFN-γ from Th1 cells activates macrophages to form epithelioid cells; giant cells attempt to contain the bacteria. |

**Connecting to Pattern Recognition:** The classic vigenette’s apical cavitation and night sweats are direct results of this pathophys. Cavitation also explains high contagiousness – cough generates aerosolised bacteria.

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| **Infectious – most common** | *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* (human) | Contact with active TB case; positive IGRA |
| **Infectious** | *M. bovis* (bovine) | Unpasteurised dairy products; cervical lymphadenopathy |
| **Reactivation triggers** | HIV infection (CD4 <350) | Low CD4 count, oral thrush, weight loss |
| **Reactivation triggers** | TNF-α inhibitors (e.g., infliximab) | Patient with rheumatoid arthritis on biologic |
| **Reactivation triggers** | Immunosuppressants (steroids, post-transplant) | Chronic steroid use, organ transplant recipient |
| **Reactivation triggers** | Malnutrition, diabetes, chronic renal failure | Poor dentition, dialysis, low albumin |
| **Reactivation triggers** | Silicosis | Occupational exposure + CXR with upper lobe nodules |
| **Latent infection** | *M. tuberculosis* – no active disease | Positive TST/IGRA, normal CXR, asymptomatic |

**Most common cause:** Reactivation of **latent *M. tuberculosis* infection** – up to 5–10% lifetime risk in immunocompetent, higher in immunosuppressed.

---

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria:**
- **Clinical (symptoms + risk factors)** + **microbiological (AFB smear, culture, molecular)**
- **Standard:** At least **2 sputum samples** for AFB smear and culture, plus chest X-ray.
- **Scoring?** Not routinely used – but **NICE guidelines** recommend culture-positive case definition.

**Key Tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Chest X-ray** | Apical infiltrates, cavitation, hilar lymphadenopathy (primary), fibrosis, pleural effusion | First-line screening; pattern recognition | Normal – does not rule out (especially HIV). Cavitation = high infectivity. Upper lobe = classic. |
| **Sputum AFB smear (Ziehl-Neelsen)** | Red acid-fast bacilli (AFB) on microscopy | Rapid result (<24h) to confirm TB and assess infectivity | + smear = high bacterial load, infectious. Negative smear ≠ not TB (paucibacillary disease). |
| **GeneXpert (MTB/RIF)** | Detects *M. tuberculosis* DNA + rifampicin resistance mutations | Molecular test with same-day result; also RIF resistance screening | Positive + RIF resistance = MDR-TB suspect. Negative = low probability but not excluded. |
| **Mycobacterial culture (liquid + solid)** | Growth of *M. tuberculosis* (2–6 weeks) | Gold standard for confirmation and full DST | Positive = definite diagnosis. DST guides therapy. |
| **Interferon-gamma release assay (IGRA)** | T-cell response to TB-specific antigens | Latent TB diagnosis; helps in screening | Positive = infection (active or latent). Negative suggests no infection (but false-negative in immunosuppression). |
| **Bronchoscopy with BAL** | Direct sampling from affected lobe | If sputum smear-negative or patient cannot expectorate | BAL smear + culture + molecular – high yield in cavitary disease. |

**Single Finding That Confirms:** **Positive culture for *M. tuberculosis*** from any respiratory specimen.

**Single Finding That Rules Out (with caveats):** Three negative sputum smears + negative GeneXpert + no clinical/CXR evidence of TB makes active TB **unlikely** but not impossible (paucibacillary, atypical TB). For latent TB, negative IGRA essentially rules out infection except in severe immunosuppression.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (First Hour):**
- **Infection control:** Isolate the patient in a **negative pressure room** (airborne precautions: N95 respirator, surgical mask on patient).
- **Obtain 3 sputum samples** (if not already done) for AFB, GeneXpert, culture.
- **Initiate therapy** *if suspicion is high* (e.g., cavitary CXR + positive smear). Do not wait for culture if risk of infectivity or severe disease.

**Ward (24–72 Hours):**
- **Start standard 4-drug regimen:**
  - **Rifampicin** 600 mg PO once daily (if <50 kg: 10 mg/kg)
  - **Isoniazid** 300 mg PO once daily (pyridoxine 25 mg co-administered to prevent neuropathy)
  - **Pyrazinamide** 1.5–2 g PO once daily (<50 kg: 30 mg/kg)
  - **Ethambutol** 15 mg/kg PO once daily
  *Duration:* 2 months (intensive phase) → then 4 months continuation (Rifampicin + Isoniazid). Total 6 months. For cavitary disease or culture-positive at 2 months → extend to 9 months.

- **Check baseline:** LFTs (Rifampicin/Isoniazid), uric acid (Pyrazinamide), visual acuity/colour vision (Ethambutol), renal function.
- **Start pyridoxine (vit B6)** 25 mg daily with Isoniazid to prevent peripheral neuropathy.
- **Advise:** All drugs taken together empty stomach (1 hour before food) – Rifampicin absorption reduced with food.

- **Monitor adherence via Directly Observed Therapy (DOT)** – especially if social risk factors, homelessness, substance use. Consider fixed-dose combination tablets (FDC) to reduce pill burden.

- **Why these drugs?** Rifampicin and Isoniazid are the two most potent bactericidal agents. Pyrazinamide works in acidic intracellular environments. Ethambutol prevents emergence of resistance during intensive phase (especially if rifampicin resistance unknown).

**Long-Term:**
- **Continuation phase (months 3–6):** Rifampicin + Isoniazid daily (or three times weekly under DOT).
- **Monitor for hepatotoxicity:** monthly LFTs, clinical signs (nausea, jaundice, dark urine). If ALT >3x normal with symptoms or >5x without, hold drugs and discuss with TB specialist.
- **Screen for peripheral neuropathy** at each visit (Isoniazid). Adjust pyridoxine dose to 50–100 mg if symptoms occur.
- **Repeat sputum culture at month 2** – if still positive, check drug sensitivity, consider extended treatment.
- **Complete treatment = 6 months (or longer if MDR, CNS involvement, etc.).**

**What NOT to do (Common Pitfalls):**
- **Use of fluoroquinolones alone** for suspected community-acquired pneumonia when TB is possible – can delay diagnosis and mask disease.
- **Do not start steroids routinely** for pulmonary TB unless tuberculosis pericarditis or severe respiratory failure (acute distress syndrome) – steroids can worsen bacterial clearance.
- **Do not use isoniazid alone** for secondary prevention in active TB – it causes rapid emergence of resistance.
- **Do not stop treatment** just because patient feels better – relapse risk ~10% if <6 months.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Pulmonary TB | Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP) | Lung Cancer | Sarcoidosis |
|---------|--------------|-------------------------------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Pain** | Usually none; pleuritic if effusion | Pleuritic chest pain common | Dull ache, may be absent | Rare; if hilar nodes, chest pressure |
| **Exam** | Dullness at apex; crackles; lymphadenopathy (supraclavicular) | Bronchial breathing, coarse crackles | Usually normal until late; clubbing | Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, skin lesions (erythema nodosum) |
| **CXR** | Apical cavity, upper lobe opacity | Lobar consolidation (air bronchogram) | Solitary nodule, mass, pleural effusion | Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, pulmonary infiltrates (Stage I–III) |
| **Symptoms** | Cough >3 weeks, night sweats, weight loss | Acute onset (<1 week), high fever, purulent sputum | Chronic cough, haemoptysis (frothy or clot), weight loss | Fatigue, dry cough, ocular symptoms, arthralgia |
| **Labs** | AFB smear +, GeneXpert +, lymphopenia, high ESR/CRP | Leukocytosis, procalcitonin elevated | Normal CBC, LDH elevated, Ca2+ may be high (paraneoplastic) | ACE level elevated (not specific), hypercalcaemia, lymphopenia |
| **Key differentiating test** | Sputum AFB + culture positive | Blood cultures, sputum Gram stain/culture | CT chest + PET, biopsy (bronchoscopy) | Lymph node biopsy (non-caseating granulomas), negative TB work-up |

**Mimics recap:** TB can be mistaken for **pneumonia** (acute vs chronic), **lung cancer** (weight loss, haemoptysis, apical mass – but TB cavity is thin-walled), and **sarcoidosis** (bilateral hilar nodes, but TB rarely causes bilateral nodes unless miliary).

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | Presentation | Key Signs | Action |
|--------------|--------------|-----------|--------|
| **Haemoptysis (massive)** | Coughing up >200 mL blood in 24h; significant airway obstruction or shock | Hypotension, tachypnoea, oxygen desaturation; CXR shows new cavitary lesion | Emergency: call bronchoscopy/IR ± embolisation. Give supplemental O2, cross-match, IV fluids. |
| **Bronchiectasis** | Chronic cough with copious purulent sputum, haemoptysis, recurrent infections | Chest crackles, clubbing, CXR: tram track lines, HRCT shows bronchiectasis | Treat exacerbations with antibiotics; consider lung resection if limited disease. |
| **Miliary TB** | Disseminated via bloodstream: high fever, hepatosplenomegaly, meningitis, choroidal tubercles | CXR: millet-sized nodules bilaterally; elevated LFTs, hyponatremia (SIADH) | Start full TB therapy immediately; consider steroids for severe CNS involvement. |
| **Tuberculous pleural effusion** | Shortness of breath, pleuritic pain, dull percussion, decreased breath sounds | CXR: blunted costophrenic angle; pleural fluid: exudative (protein >3 g/dL), lymphocytic (>50%), ADA high | Diagnostic thoracentesis – start TB therapy; steroids not indicated unless adhesions. |
| **Laryngeal TB** | Hoarseness, odynophagia, ear pain (referred from vagus nerve) | Laryngoscopy shows mucosal ulcerations or granulomas | Ask about TB symptoms; treat as pulmonary TB; obtain sputum for AFB. |
| **TB meningitis** (late, untreated) | Headache, fever, neck stiffness, drowsiness, cranial nerve palsies (especially VI) | CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, low glucose, high protein | Urgent – start TB therapy + dexamethasone; LP confirms. High mortality if delayed. |
| **Drug-induced hepatitis** | Nausea, jaundice, dark urine, RUQ pain | LFTs: ALT >3x ULN + symptoms | Hold all hepatotoxic drugs (Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide) and discuss with TB specialist; alternative regimen needed. |

**Late presentation pattern:** Untreated pulmonary TB steadily worsens – weight loss becomes cachexia, haemoptysis can be fatal (Rasmussen aneurysm from cavitation eroding a pulmonary artery branch), and miliary spread leads to multiorgan failure.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic – RIPE for first-line drugs:**  
- **R**ifampicin  
- **I**soniazid  
- **P**yrazinamide  
- **E**thambutol  

**Mnemonic – SIDE effects of TB drugs:**
- **R**ifampicin: **R**ed urine, **R**ash, **R**ise in LFTs, **R**apid metabolism of other drugs (war, OCP, etc.)
- **I**soniazid: **I**nflammation of liver, **I**nsufficiency of pyridoxine → neuropathy
- **P**yrazinamide: **P**ain in joints (hyperuricemia)
- **E**thambutol: **E**yes – optic neuritis (test colour vision monthly)

**Mnemonic – Who to screen for latent TB?**
- **P**rison inmates
- **H**IV-positive
- **I**mmigrant from high-prevalence country
- **S**ilicosis
- **C**ontact of active case
- **T**NF-alpha inhibitor candidates

**High-Yield Clinical Pearls:**
1. **"Think TB when cough >3 weeks + any risk factor."** Even without classic symptoms.
2. **Cavitation on CXR is almost always TB until proven otherwise** (especially in young adults). Lung cancer cavities are thick-walled with irregular margins.
3. **Negative sputum AFB does not rule out TB** – especially in HIV, children, or extra-pulmonary disease. Send GeneXpert.
4. **Check for drug interactions with Rifampicin:** It potently induces CYP3A4, reducing levels of warfarin, oral contraceptives, methadone, antiretrovirals.
5. **Pyridoxine is mandatory with Isoniazid** to prevent peripheral neuropathy – but dose of 10–25 mg/day is enough for prophylaxis.
6. **Do not stop rifampicin without discussing with TB specialist** – it is the cornerstone of treatment and resistance can be missed.
7. **Weight-based dosing** is essential for Ethambutol and Pyrazinamide – avoid fixed doses in extremes of weight (<50 kg or >80 kg).

**Common Exam / Ward Mistakes to Avoid:**
- **Ordering TB tests without clinical suspicion** (low yield – leads to false positives in low-prevalence settings)
- **Using steroids alone for TB pericarditis** without anti-TB therapy
- **Starting quinolone monotherapy** for a COPD exacerbation (may suppress AFB smears and delay culture)
- **Failing to isolate the patient** when AFB smear-positive – risk of nosocomial spread

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

| Scenario | Threshold | Action |
|----------|-----------|--------|
| **Massive haemoptysis** | >200 mL in 24h or airway compromise + haemodynamic instability | Call anaesthetics/ICU, bronchoscopy team, interventional radiology for bronchial artery embolisation. |
| **Respiratory failure** | SpO2 <90% on room air, NEWS2 ≥5, PaO2 <8 kPa on ABG (type 1/2) | High-flow O2, consider NIV if hypercapnoeic, transfer to ICU. |
| **TB meningitis** | New confusion, GCS ≤14, seizure, or focal neurology | Urgent CT head + LP. Start dexamethasone + anti-TB; refer to neurology/infectious disease; ICU if reduced consciousness. |
| **Drug-induced hepatitis** | ALT >5x ULN (even asymptomatic) or >3x ULN with symptoms (nausea, jaundice) | Stop all drugs, check INR. Contact TB specialist. Replace with alternative regimen (e.g., streptomycin, levofloxacin, etc.). May need admission. |
| **Sepsis (miliary TB)** | NEWS2 ≥7, lactate >2 mmol/L, hypotension not responding to fluid | Full sepsis protocol, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (while awaiting TB results). Start TB therapy as soon as suspected. ICU review. |
| **Hepatotoxicity requiring hold** | Any sign of jaundice or ALT rising >5x ULN | Call senior clinician, stop hepatotoxic agents immediately, arrange daily LFTs, discuss with ID/microbiology. |

**Key thresholds:**  
- NEWS2 >5 triggers escalation review.  
- GCS <12 triggers CT head and consideration for ICU.  
- Lactate >4 mmol/L in any setting (especially with miliary pattern) indicates severe sepsis.  
- ALT >200 U/L = immediate hepatotoxicity work-up.

**Always communicate:** “I have a patient with suspected/confirmed TB who is deteriorating due to [complication]. I need a senior review within [minutes] because [specific parameter] is compromised.”
